common_name: Noble Muscadine Grape (Large Dark-Purple Self-Fertile Gulf South Muscadine)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun; Noble has perfect (self-fertile) flowers — it will set fruit without a pollinator but produces better crops when planted with another muscadine cultivar nearby. Provide a sturdy trellis, arbor, or fence; muscadines are vigorous climbers.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun; Noble is one of the most widely planted and highly regarded purple muscadine grape cultivars for Gulf South home gardens and small vineyards — a vigorous, productive vine producing large (1–1.5 inch), round, deep purple-black to dark purple grapes with thick, slightly tough skin and sweet, musky, richly flavored flesh that is the definition of classic muscadine flavor. Muscadine grapes (Muscadinia rotundifolia) are the grape of the American Southeast — a separate species from European and California wine grapes, native to the coastal plain from Florida to Virginia, and extraordinarily well-adapted to the heat, humidity, and disease pressure of Gulf South summers that devastate Vitis vinifera varieties. Noble bears perfect (hermaphroditic) flowers and is self-fertile, a major advantage over the older cultivars that required a male or bisexual pollinator; it is also an excellent pollinator for female-flowered cultivars like Fry and Higgins. The large grape clusters ripen in August–September and are harvested individually as grapes ripen separately within each cluster — a distinctive feature of muscadine harvesting. Noble is used for fresh eating, juice, jelly, wine, and vinegar. The thick skin and seeds are the main characteristics distinguishing muscadine from table grapes — some people find them unpleasant, while others consider them inseparable from the authentic flavor experience. Noble wine produces a characteristically sweet, richly flavored muscadine wine very different from European wine styles.

common_name: Carlos Muscadine Grape (Classic Bronze Self-Fertile Wine-Quality Gulf South Muscadine)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun; Carlos has perfect (self-fertile) flowers and is one of the best pollinators for female-flowered muscadine cultivars — planting Carlos near female cultivars consistently improves production in mixed plantings.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun; Carlos is the benchmark bronze muscadine cultivar — one of the most widely planted and historically important Gulf South muscadines, valued equally for fresh eating quality and as the primary variety for muscadine wine production in the Southeast. The medium to large, round grapes ripen to bronze-tan to bronze-gold color with thick skin and sweet, aromatic, musky flesh typical of the muscadine type; the flavor profile is somewhat more refined and balanced in acidity than many other muscadine cultivars, which is why Carlos is the preferred variety for wine production and has long been the standard against which other muscadines are evaluated. Muscadine wine from Carlos has a characteristic sweet, floral, musky quality — distinctively Southern and quite unlike any European wine style — with enough natural acidity to provide balance. Carlos is self-fertile with perfect flowers and is an outstanding cross-pollinator for female-flowered cultivars including Fry, Higgins, and others. The vines are vigorous and productive, disease-resistant to the fungal diseases (black rot, downy mildew, powdery mildew) that make growing European grapes nearly impossible in Gulf South conditions, and adapted to the heat, humidity, and long growing season of zones 6–10. Grapes ripen in August through September, with individual grapes in each cluster ripening sequentially. Excellent for fresh eating, juice, muscadine wine, jelly, and preserves.

common_name: Hurricane Lily (Red Spider Lily, Classic Fall-Blooming Surprise Lily for Gulf South)

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall (August–October) with the neck of the bulb at or just below soil surface in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; do not divide bulbs more than necessary — clusters that have not been disturbed for several years produce the most spectacular flowering displays.

directions: Plant bulbs in late summer–fall in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; the hurricane lily (Lycoris radiata) is one of the most distinctive and eagerly anticipated flowers of the Gulf South fall calendar — a surprise bulb that sends up naked, leafless flower stalks seemingly overnight in late September, producing umbels of intensely red, spidery flowers with dramatically long, curving stamens and petals that reflex backward, creating a starbursting, spider-like form unique among commonly grown bulbs. The common name 'hurricane lily' reflects the Gulf Coast tradition of these blooms appearing around hurricane season in September–October; 'surprise lily' comes from the way they emerge from bare soil with no prior visible foliage to signal their presence. The unusual growth cycle — green strap-like leaves emerge after the flowers die in fall, persist through winter and spring as a ground-level groundcover, then go completely dormant in early summer leaving no trace — means the bulbs are frequently disrupted by gardeners unaware of their presence during summer dormancy. Planting bulbs in a location marked with a stake or near a permanent plant prevents accidental disturbance. Undisturbed colonies naturalize and expand into impressive masses of red over many years; the best displays are typically seen in old Southern gardens where colonies have been undisturbed for decades. The bulbs are toxic if ingested. Excellent under deciduous trees in partial shade where the summer dormancy coincides with leafed-out canopy.

common_name: Atamasco Lily (Native Southeast White Rain Lily for Moist Woodland Gardens)

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 1–2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Atamasco lily naturally grows in moist, seasonally wet areas — along stream banks, low areas, and moist woodland edges — and performs best in garden situations that provide consistent moisture, particularly in spring during its growth and bloom period.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall 1–2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Atamasco Lily is a native southeastern bulb producing exquisitely beautiful, large (2.5–3.5 inch), funnel-shaped flowers of pure glistening white sometimes tinged with pale lavender-pink, emerging on leafless stalks from March through April following spring rains — one of the finest native wildflowers of the coastal plain Southeast and a stunning addition to naturalistic and woodland gardens throughout its native range. The flowers have the clean, pure-white simplicity of a fine amaryllis in miniature, each bloom emerging individually on an upright, hollow stem surrounded by the narrow, grass-like leaves that emerge simultaneously with the flowers in late winter and persist into summer. Unlike many exotic rain lilies, Atamasco lily is indigenous to moist forests, stream banks, and wet meadows from Virginia to Florida and west to Mississippi — a native plant that evolved in Gulf Coast conditions and thrives without supplemental care in suitable moist sites. In the wild, it frequently grows in such large populations in moist flatwoods and lowland areas that spring bloomings create sweeping drifts of white through the forest understory. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested. Excellent naturalized in moist lawn areas, along stream banks, and in woodland garden understory positions where its preference for consistent spring moisture can be met naturally.

common_name: Pink Rain Lily (Caribbean Rosy-Pink Summer Rain-Triggered Zephyranthes)

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Pink Rain Lily responds to the rain and humidity of Gulf South summer storms with explosive simultaneous blooming — the flowers appear 3–5 days after heavy rain events, with all blooms opening within a short window before closing and fading.

directions: Plant bulbs in spring 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Pink Rain Lily (Zephyranthes rosea) is a Caribbean bulb from Cuba and other West Indian islands, producing small (1.5–2 inch), clear rose-pink to warm deep-pink funnel-shaped flowers that appear in coordinated, simultaneous flushes days after summer rain — a uniquely responsive blooming trigger that makes rain lily displays brief, intense, and tied directly to Gulf South summer storm events. The pink color of Z. rosea is warmer and deeper than the blush-tinged white of the native Atamasco lily, providing a more vivid summer color accent; the genus name Zephyranthes means 'flower of the west wind', reflecting the traditional association of these bulbs with rains coming from storm systems. In Gulf South zone 9, Pink Rain Lily is one of the most reliable and easily naturalized of all small summer bulbs — it multiplies freely by offsets to form dense clumps that bloom repeatedly through summer and fall, naturalizing in lawn edges, rock gardens, and perennial border fronts. The narrow, rush-like leaves appear with or before the flowers and remain through summer. Excellent naturalized in drifts under partially shaded garden beds where summer rainfall triggers reliable repeat bloom. Bulb clumps can be divided every 3–4 years when overcrowding reduces bloom, though undisturbed clumps often bloom more reliably.
